(born **Imre József Pressburger**; 5 December 1902 – 5 February 1988) was a Hungarian-British screenwriter, film director, and producer. He is best known for his series of film collaborations with [Michael Powell](/wiki/Michael_Powell "Michael Powell"), in a collaboration partnership known as [the Archers](/wiki/Powell_and_Pressburger "Powell and Pressburger"), and produced a series of films, including *[49th Parallel](/wiki/49th_Parallel_(film) "49th Parallel (film)")* (US: *The Invaders*, 1941), *[The Life and Death of Colonel Blimp](/wiki/The_Life_and_Death_of_Colonel_Blimp "The Life and Death of Colonel Blimp")* (1943), *[A Matter of Life and Death](/wiki/A_Matter_of_Life_and_Death_(1946_film) "A Matter of Life and Death (1946 film)")* (US: *Stairway to Heaven*, 1946), *[Black Narcissus](/wiki/Black_Narcissus "Black Narcissus")* (1947), *[The Red Shoes](/wiki/The_Red_Shoes_(1948_film) "The Red Shoes (1948 film)")* (1948), and *[The Tales of Hoffmann](/wiki/The_Tales_of_Hoffmann_(1951_film) "The Tales of Hoffmann (1951 film)")* (1951).

**Emeric Pressburger** (born **Imre József Pressburger**; 5 December 1902 – 5 February 1988) was a Hungarian-British screenwriter, film director, and producer. He is best known for his series of film collaborations with [Michael Powell](/wiki/Michael_Powell "Michael Powell"), in a collaboration partnership known as [the Archers](/wiki/Powell_and_Pressburger "Powell and Pressburger"), and produced a series of films, including *[49th Parallel](/wiki/49th_Parallel_(film) "49th Parallel (film)")* (US: *The Invaders*, 1941), *[The Life and Death of Colonel Blimp](/wiki/The_Life_and_Death_of_Colonel_Blimp "The Life and Death of Colonel Blimp")* (1943), *[A Matter of Life and Death](/wiki/A_Matter_of_Life_and_Death_(1946_film) "A Matter of Life and Death (1946 film)")* (US: *Stairway to Heaven*, 1946), *[Black Narcissus](/wiki/Black_Narcissus "Black Narcissus")* (1947), *[The Red Shoes](/wiki/The_Red_Shoes_(1948_film) "The Red Shoes (1948 film)")* (1948), and *[The Tales of Hoffmann](/wiki/The_Tales_of_Hoffmann_(1951_film) "The Tales of Hoffmann (1951 film)")* (1951).

## Early years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Emeric_Pressburger&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early years")]

Imre József Pressburger was born in [Miskolc](/wiki/Miskolc "Miskolc"), in the [Kingdom of Hungary](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Hungary "Kingdom of Hungary"), of [Jewish](/wiki/Jew "Jew") heritage.[[1]](#cite_note-Behr1-1) He was the only son (he had one elder half-sister from his father's previous marriage) of Kálmán Pressburger, estate manager, and his second wife, Kätherina (née Wichs). He attended a boarding-school in [Temesvár](/wiki/Timi%C8%99oara "Timișoara") (today better known as Timișoara), where he was a good pupil, excelling at mathematics, literature and music. He then studied mathematics and engineering at the Universities of [Prague](/wiki/Charles_University_in_Prague "Charles University in Prague") and [Stuttgart](/wiki/University_of_Stuttgart "University of Stuttgart") before his father's death forced him to abandon his studies.[[2]](#cite_note-Macdonald-2)

## Film career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Emeric_Pressburger&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Film career")]

### Berlin and Paris

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Emeric_Pressburger&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Berlin and Paris")]

Pressburger began a career as a journalist. After working in Hungary and [Weimar Republic](/wiki/Weimar_Republic "Weimar Republic")-era Germany he turned to screenwriting in the late 1920s, working for [UFA](/wiki/UFA_GmbH "UFA GmbH") in [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin "Berlin") (having moved there in 1926). The rise of the [Nazis](/wiki/Nazism "Nazism") forced him to flee to Paris, where he again worked as screenwriter, and then to London. He later said, "[the] worst things that happened to me were the political consequences of events beyond my control ... the best things were exactly the same."

Pressburger's early films were mainly made in Germany and France where he worked at the UFA Studios in the Dramaturgie department (script selection, approval and editing) and as a scriptwriter in his own right. In the 1930s, many European films were produced in multiple-language versions. Some of the films made in Germany survive with French intertitles and vice versa.

In 1933, after the Nazis came to power, UFA's head sacked the company's remaining Jewish employees with Pressburger being told his contract would not be renewed. He left his Berlin apartment, "leaving the key in the door so that the [Stormtroopers](/wiki/Sturmabteilung "Sturmabteilung") wouldn't have to break the door down" and left for Paris. Late in 1935, Pressburger decided that he would do better in England.

### Emigration to the United Kingdom

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Emeric_Pressburger&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Emigration to the United Kingdom")]

Pressburger arrived in Britain in 1935 as a stateless person; once he decided to settle, he changed his name to Emeric in 1938. In England, he found a small community of Hungarian film-makers who had fled the [Nazis](/wiki/Nazism "Nazism"), including [Alexander Korda](/wiki/Alexander_Korda "Alexander Korda"), owner of [London Films](/wiki/London_Films "London Films"), who employed him as a screenwriter. Asked by Korda to improve the script for *[The Spy in Black](/wiki/The_Spy_in_Black "The Spy in Black")* (1939), he met the film's director, [Michael Powell](/wiki/Michael_Powell "Michael Powell"). [Their partnership](/wiki/Powell_and_Pressburger "Powell and Pressburger") would produce some of the most acclaimed British films of the next decade.[[3]](#cite_note-Christie-3) However, Pressburger still did some projects on his own.

Pressburger was much more than "Michael Powell's screenwriter" as some have categorised him. The films they made together in this period were mainly original stories by Pressburger, who also did most of the work of a producer for the team. Pressburger was also more involved in the editing process than Powell, and as a musician, Pressburger was also involved in the choice of music for their films.

### Later work

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Emeric_Pressburger&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Later work")]

Powell and Pressburger began to go their separate ways after the mid-1950s. They remained close friends but wanted to explore different things, having done about as much as they could together. Their last film as a partnership was *Ill Met By Moonlight* but Pressburger's first film as a solo producer, *Miracle in Soho*, flopped.[[4]](#cite_note-seven-4)

Two of his later films were made under the pseudonym "Richard Imrie".

Two novels by Pressburger were published. The first *Killing a Mouse on a Sunday* (1961), is set in the period immediately following the [Spanish Civil War](/wiki/Spanish_Civil_War "Spanish Civil War"). It received favourable reviews and was soon translated into a dozen languages. His second novel, *The Glass Pearls* (1966), reissued in 2015 and again in 2022 by [Faber](/wiki/Faber_and_Faber "Faber and Faber"), gained an especially negative assessment from *The Times Literary Supplement*, its only contemporary review.[[5]](#cite_note-5) Subsequently, it has been highly praised. Lucy Scholes in *[The Paris Review](/wiki/The_Paris_Review "The Paris Review")* in 2019 called it "a truly remarkable work. It deserves to be recognized both for its own virtuosity, and as an important addition to the genre of Holocaust literature."[[6]](#cite_note-6)

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Emeric_Pressburger&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Personal life")]

On 24 June 1938, Pressburger married [Ági Donáth](/wiki/%C3%81gi_Don%C3%A1th "Ági Donáth"), the daughter of Andor Donáth, a general merchant, but they divorced in 1941. The union was childless. He remarried, on 29 March 1947, to Wendy Orme, and they had a daughter, Angela, and another child who died as a baby in 1948; but this marriage also ended in divorce in [Reno, Nevada](/wiki/Reno,_Nevada "Reno, Nevada") in 1953 and in Britain in 1971. His daughter Angela's two sons both became successful film-makers: [Andrew Macdonald](/wiki/Andrew_Macdonald_(producer) "Andrew Macdonald (producer)") as a producer on films such as *[Trainspotting](/wiki/Trainspotting_(movie) "Trainspotting (movie)")* (1996), and [Kevin Macdonald](/wiki/Kevin_Macdonald_(director) "Kevin Macdonald (director)") as an [Oscar](/wiki/Academy_Awards "Academy Awards")-winning director. Kevin has written a biography of his grandfather, and a documentary about his life, *The Making of an Englishman* (1995).

Pressburger became a British citizen in 1946. He was made a Fellow of [BAFTA](/wiki/British_Academy_of_Film_and_Television_Arts "British Academy of Film and Television Arts") in 1981, and a Fellow of the [BFI](/wiki/British_Film_Institute "British Film Institute") in 1983.

Pressburger was a diffident and private person who, at times, particularly later on in his life, could be hypersensitive and prone to bouts of melancholia. He loved [French cuisine](/wiki/French_cuisine "French cuisine"), enjoyed music, and possessed a great sense of humour. In appearance he was short, wore glasses, and had a sagacious, bird-like facial expression. He was a keen supporter of [Arsenal F.C.](/wiki/Arsenal_F.C. "Arsenal F.C."), a passion he developed soon after arriving in Britain. From 1970 he lived in [Aspall, Suffolk](/wiki/Aspall,_Suffolk "Aspall, Suffolk")[[7]](#cite_note-address-7) and he died in a [nursing home](/wiki/Nursing_home "Nursing home") in nearby [Saxtead](/wiki/Saxtead "Saxtead") on 5 February 1988. He is interred in the cemetery of St. Mary of Grace Church, Aspall.[[7]](#cite_note-address-7) His is the only grave in that [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England") graveyard with a [Star of David](/wiki/Star_of_David "Star of David").

## Filmography

## Awards, nominations and honours

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Emeric_Pressburger&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Awards, nominations and honours")]

* 1943: [Oscar](/wiki/Academy_Award "Academy Award") winner for *[49th Parallel](/wiki/49th_Parallel_(film) "49th Parallel (film)")* as Best Writing, Original Story. (This Oscar is on display at the [Savile Club](/wiki/Savile_Club "Savile Club") in [London](/wiki/London "London")).[[8]](#cite_note-8)
* 1943: [Oscar](/wiki/Academy_Award "Academy Award") nominated for *[49th Parallel](/wiki/49th_Parallel_(film) "49th Parallel (film)")* as Best Writing, Screenplay. Shared with [Rodney Ackland](/wiki/Rodney_Ackland "Rodney Ackland")
* 1943: [Oscar](/wiki/Academy_Award "Academy Award") nominated for *[One of Our Aircraft Is Missing](/wiki/One_of_Our_Aircraft_Is_Missing "One of Our Aircraft Is Missing")* for Best Writing, Original Screenplay. Shared with [Michael Powell](/wiki/Michael_Powell "Michael Powell")
* 1948: Won Danish [Bodil Award](/wiki/Bodil_Award "Bodil Award") for *[A Matter of Life and Death](/wiki/A_Matter_of_Life_and_Death_(1946_film) "A Matter of Life and Death (1946 film)")* as Best European Film. Shared with Michael Powell
* 1948: Nominated for *[The Red Shoes](/wiki/The_Red_Shoes_(1948_film) "The Red Shoes (1948 film)")* for [Venice Film Festival](/wiki/Venice_Film_Festival "Venice Film Festival") Golden Lion. Shared with Michael Powell
* 1949: [Oscar](/wiki/Academy_Award "Academy Award") nominated for *[The Red Shoes](/wiki/The_Red_Shoes_(1948_film) "The Red Shoes (1948 film)")* as Best Picture. Shared with Michael Powell
* 1949: [Oscar](/wiki/Academy_Award "Academy Award") nominated for *[The Red Shoes](/wiki/The_Red_Shoes_(1948_film) "The Red Shoes (1948 film)")* as Best Writing, Motion Picture Story
* 1951: [Cannes Film Festival](/wiki/1951_Cannes_Film_Festival "1951 Cannes Film Festival") nominated for *[The Tales of Hoffmann](/wiki/The_Tales_of_Hoffmann_(film) "The Tales of Hoffmann (film)")* for Grand Prize of the Festival. Shared with Michael Powell
* 1951: Won [Silver Bear](/wiki/Silver_Bear "Silver Bear") from [1st Berlin International Film Festival](/wiki/1st_Berlin_International_Film_Festival "1st Berlin International Film Festival") for *[The Tales of Hoffmann](/wiki/The_Tales_of_Hoffmann_(film) "The Tales of Hoffmann (film)")* as Best Musical. Shared with Michael Powell[[9]](#cite_note-Berlinale_1951-9)
* 1957: [BAFTA Award](/wiki/BAFTA_Award "BAFTA Award") nominated for *[The Battle of the River Plate](/wiki/The_Battle_of_the_River_Plate_(film) "The Battle of the River Plate (film)")* as Best British Screenplay. Shared with Michael Powell
* 1981: Made fellow of [BAFTA](/wiki/British_Academy_of_Film_and_Television_Arts "British Academy of Film and Television Arts")
* 1983: Made fellow of the [British Film Institute](/wiki/British_Film_Institute "British Film Institute") (BFI)
* 2014: An [English Heritage](/wiki/English_Heritage "English Heritage") [Blue plaque](/wiki/Blue_plaque "Blue plaque") to commemorate Michael Powell and Emeric Pressburger was unveiled on 17 February 2014 by [Martin Scorsese](/wiki/Martin_Scorsese "Martin Scorsese") and [Thelma Schoonmaker](/wiki/Thelma_Schoonmaker "Thelma Schoonmaker") at [Dorset House](/wiki/Dorset_House,_Marylebone "Dorset House, Marylebone"), [Gloucester Place](/wiki/Gloucester_Place "Gloucester Place"), London NW1 5AG where The Archers had their offices from 1942 to 1947.
